---
title: "Phase 1: Data Foundation"
subtitle: "Kikoff Marketing Mix Model"
date: 2026-04-28
author: "Data Science Team"
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    theme: default
    transition: none
    width: 1200
    height: 800
    center: false
    embed-resources: true
    execute:
      echo: false
      warning: false
---

## We're building two parallel models to answer Kikoff's budget allocation question {.smaller}

**Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM):** attributes conversions and revenue to each channel, controlling for seasonality and ad carryover effects.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Conversion model**

- *Question:* How many customers per channel?
- *Data:* Daily conversion count
- *Role:* **Primary** — directly observed
- *Use:* iCAC, budget allocation anchor
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Revenue model**

- *Question:* How valuable were those customers?
- *Data:* Daily cohort LTV (Kikoff's 1-year predicted revenue)
- *Role:* **Directional / supplementary**
- *Use:* Channel quality check
:::
:::

## Channels that look equal on conversions can differ sharply on customer value

**Why conversion counts alone are insufficient:**

- A volume DSP drives more signups — but those customers churn quickly → lower LTV
- Meta drives fewer signups — but customers stay longer and engage more → higher LTV
- A single conversion model treats these as equivalent → wrong budget recommendation

**What the revenue model adds:**

The quality dimension. Meta's lower conversion volume becomes the right recommendation once you see it drives higher-value customers.

## How we use both models — and what to do if they disagree {.smaller}

| | Conversion Model | Revenue Model |
|---|---|---|
| Primary use | iCAC, budget allocation | Channel quality check |
| Reliability | **High** — directly observed | Moderate — Kikoff's LTV model is the source |
| Recent cohorts | Stable | Noisier (less behavioral data) |
| If they agree | Strong signal | High confidence |
| **If they diverge** | **Flag it to Kikoff** | **Divergence = informative finding** |

**Important caveats:**

- LTV_1YEAR is Kikoff's own prediction, not a direct observation — our MMM inherits any biases in it
- When the two models disagree on a channel, we surface the conflict rather than averaging it away

---

## 639 days of clean data — Q3 2024 through March 2026

![](../outputs/P1_02_eda/figures/conversions_and_revenue.png){.r-stretch}

- **Q3 2024 start:** channel-level spend is complete from this point (earlier quarters have gaps)
- **March 2026 end:** April is a partial month at export time
- **0 null LTV values** across the full 639-day modeling window

---

## Top 3 channels — Meta, TikTok, Google — account for 73% of total spend

![](../outputs/P1_02_eda/figures/top_10_channels_spend.png){.r-stretch}

**$112.9M total spend** · $132.5K daily average · 13 channels in scope

## Channel grouping decisions — and why "Others" is no longer a concern {.smaller}

| Decision | Result | Why |
|---|---|---|
| StackAdapt + Appvertiser → `dsp_combined` | One combined DSP channel | Both are programmatic buyers (Abheek's direction) |
| Bing → dropped | Excluded | Kikoff no longer advertising there |
| Others → resolved | 9.2% → ~0.0% of spend | Data engineering removed 942 erroneous rows |

**Why the Others resolution matters:** In the original file, Others peaked at 16.3% of modeling-window spend — large enough to absorb attribution that should go to real channels. With the updated file, Others = $33.9K (~0.0%). Every dollar is now tied to a trackable channel.

![](../outputs/P1_02_eda/figures/channel_composition_monthly.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

---

## Platform label corrections — two confirmed, one open {.smaller}

**The raw spend file had three inconsistent platform labels:**

| Raw label | → Clean label | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| `iso` | → `ios` | Consistent typo |
| `web and app` | → `web` | Abheek confirmed, Meeting 3 |
| `iOS and android` | → 50/50 iOS / Android | ⚠️ **Open — awaiting Kikoff confirmation** |

::: {style="background-color: #fff3cd; border-left: 4px solid #ffc107; padding: 0.5em 1em; margin-top: 0.5em;"}
**Open item:** iOS/Android 50/50 split is unconfirmed. Does not affect channel-level totals — only the platform-level breakdown within mobile.
:::

## Four days had artificially suppressed LTV — not real business behavior {.smaller}

**What happened:** LTV_1YEAR gets revised as early customer signals arrive. If data is exported before the revision, some days show artificially low LTV — not a real drop in customer value, just a timing artifact.

| Window | Dates | In modeling window? | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Window 1 | Feb 29 – Mar 11, 2024 | **No** | Documented only |
| Window 2 | Oct 21 – Oct 24, 2025 | **Yes** | **Fixed** |

**Why it matters:** Without a fix, the model would try to explain the artificial dip using marketing spend from those days — incorrectly penalizing or crediting channels for a data artifact.

## Choosing the right imputation method {.smaller}

| Method | Why it falls short |
|---|---|
| Linear interpolation | Straight-line assumption — misses day-of-week seasonality |
| Forward fill | Copies last value; biases if LTV is trending |
| Backward fill | Same problem in reverse |
| Global mean | Ignores seasonal structure (October ≠ January) |
| Boundary averaging *(current approach)* | Simple and valid; less robust if boundary days are also elevated |
| **Local trend — rolling median *(selected)*** | Draws from 14 surrounding days; robust to adjacent outliers; adapts to seasonality |

**Key principle:** Detection threshold is 3σ — only 0.3% chance of occurring naturally. Imputation is surgical, not a broad smoothing operation.

## The fix worked — post-imputation variation is within normal range

![](../outputs/P1_02_eda/figures/ltv_anomaly_spot_check.png){.r-stretch}

| | Before fix | After fix |
|---|---|---|
| Z-score (deviation from local context) | 2.81 — anomalous | **0.85 — normal range ✓** |
| Gate status | — | **PASS** |

---

## Spend and conversions move together — confirming the model has signal to learn from

![](../outputs/P1_02_eda/figures/spend_vs_conversions_scatter.png){.r-stretch}

**Sanity check before modeling:** If spend and conversions were uncorrelated, the MMM would have nothing to learn from. r = 0.445 confirms a real, positive relationship. Imperfect correlation is expected — seasonality and brand effects add noise that the MMM separates out.

**639 days · 24 channel-platform columns · 0 null LTV values**

## Tax season demand is real — and modeled separately from channel effects

![](../outputs/P1_02_eda/figures/monthly_seasonality.png){.r-stretch}

**April peaks at ~1.5× above December's trough — a consistent spring lift, likely anchored by tax season.** This is calendar-driven demand, not a channel effect. We model it explicitly so channel contributions reflect true incremental impact — not seasonal calendar lift.

---

## Four holdout tests anchor our Bayesian priors — one treated as high uncertainty {.smaller}

**A Bayesian prior** is a starting assumption the model holds before seeing data. Holdout experiments give us data-backed priors rather than guesses.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Tests used:**

| Channel | Test period | Type | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| TikTok | Aug–Sep 2025 | Holdout | High |
| Meta | May 2025 | Holdout | High |
| CTV | Oct–Nov 2025 | Geo holdout | Moderate |
| Meta | Jan 2026 | Holdout | ⚠️ Low |

Priors are **time-limited to test windows only.**

⚠️ *Jan 2026 Meta test cancelled after 3 days — cell design flaw caused a conversion volume drop. Results are directionally valid but treated as wide, diffuse priors (high uncertainty).*
:::
::: {.column width="55%"}
**iCAC from each test:**

| Channel | Platform | iCAC ($) |
|---|---|---|
| TikTok | iOS | 108.83 |
| TikTok | Android | 81.68 |
| TikTok | Web | 112.12 |
| Meta (May 2025) | iOS | 135.48 |
| Meta (May 2025) | Android | 63.06 |
| Meta (May 2025) | Web | 156.89 |
| CTV | All | 135.00 |
| Meta (Jan 2026) ⚠️ | iOS | 1,350.00 |
| Meta (Jan 2026) ⚠️ | Android | 154.52 |
| Meta (Jan 2026) ⚠️ | Web | 239.75 |
:::
:::

---

## All 5 data quality checks passed — one open assumption remaining {.smaller}

| Check | Result |
|---|---|
| LTV anomaly imputation (Oct 21–24, 2025) | ✓ PASS — z-score = 0.85 after fix |
| Platform label normalization | ✓ PASS |
| Channel taxonomy mapping | ✓ PASS |
| DSP combined coverage | ✓ PASS — 341 days in window |
| Modeling window filter | ✓ PASS — 2024-07-01 to 2026-03-31 |

**One open item:** iOS/Android 50/50 split — not yet confirmed by Kikoff. Does not block the model build.

## Phase 2: Incorporating experimental evidence into the model {.smaller}

**The core challenge:** The TikTok, Meta, and CTV holdout tests each ran for 9–28 days within a 639-day history. How do we make the model use that experimental evidence *only for the period it covers* — without assuming it applies to all 18 months?

**Three mechanisms evaluated:**

| Approach | Plain English | Our assessment |
|---|---|---|
| Time-varying effectiveness | Allow channel effectiveness to drift over time as a smooth curve | **Not viable** — curve is shared across all channels; destroys per-channel precision |
| Indicator-gated priors | Binary switch: tight, experiment-anchored assumption during test window; loose assumption outside | Most precise; requires fully custom code; fallback if timeline distinction is challenged |
| **Native lift test API** *(starting point)* | Add holdout results as calibration constraints on the spend-outcome curve, per channel | **Starting here** — battle-tested, documented, per-channel |

**Why we're starting with the native API:** It incorporates experimental evidence in a principled way without custom code risk. We'll verify post-fit that the holdout results are actually influencing the model — if not, we move to indicator-gated priors.

**After that:** Run the model → generate iCAC + response curves per channel × platform → decisioning layer. Full channel coverage targeted by early June.

---

*Phase 1 Data Foundation | Kikoff Marketing Mix Model | 2026-04-28*
